text: Shown here is a posterior cervical decompression and fusion surgery.
It essentially is done when patients have spinal cord compression, fractures, scoliosis, or arthritis.
What we do is we take off the posterior elements of the spine.
This creates inherent instability, so then it needs to be backed up with a fusion procedure with rods and screws to maintain the spine.
Once the posterior elements are removed, the spinal cord is free to essentially breathe.
It has more room in the spinal cavity to be able to flow the spinal fluid much better.
